title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The Future of the Plate: Meat and Morality

texts.A: The environmental argument for a plant-based diet is scientifically ironclad. Industrial livestock farming is a leading cause of deforestation and methane emissions. [37]While some advocate for 'lab-grown' meat as a middle ground, I believe this is a technological distraction from the simpler, more ethical path of veganism.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the idea that we can 'humanely' slaughter a sentient being is a moral contradiction that our society must eventually confront.[/39] The scientific evidence linking high meat consumption to both climate change and non-communicable diseases such as heart disease is now overwhelming and cannot be responsibly dismissed. Societies that have already shifted toward predominantly plant-based diets report lower healthcare costs and significantly smaller per-capita carbon footprints. We don't need a better way to produce meat; we need a better way to think about our relationship with other species. The argument that veganism is a niche lifestyle choice is no longer credible; it is a moral imperative demanded by the scale of the ecological crisis we face.
texts.B: Cultivated or 'lab-grown' meat represents the most significant culinary breakthrough of the century. It offers the experience of eating animal protein without the associated suffering or environmental degradation. [38]I disagree with those who claim that it is 'unnatural'; everything we eat, from pasteurized milk to hybrid crops, is a product of human intervention.[/38] [40]The real hurdle is not the technology, but the cost; until lab-grown steaks are as cheap as factory-farmed ones, the average consumer will not make the switch.[/40] [37]I share the view that this technology is the only realistic way to satisfy the global demand for meat without destroying the planet.[/37] Public investment is therefore critical—just as subsidies once made conventional agriculture affordable for billions, governments must now direct funding toward scaling cultivated protein. The historical precedent is clear: when new food technologies receive sustained institutional support, price parity follows within a generation. Critics who dismiss lab-grown meat as a vanity project misunderstand both the urgency of the climate crisis and the remarkable speed at which this technology is advancing.
texts.C: The push toward synthetic meat ignores the vital role that regenerative grazing plays in healthy ecosystems. [38]Unlike those who see all meat production as an environmental disaster, I would argue that well-managed livestock can actually restore soil health and sequester carbon.[/38] The problem is not the cow, but the system. Furthermore, the 'high-tech' solutions offered by Silicon Valley are often just another way for corporations to gain control over the food chain. [39]I am less convinced than some that a lab-grown nugget is 'morally superior' if its production requires an energy-intensive industrial process that detaches us even further from nature.[/39] Small-scale, pasture-based farming represents not only an ecological solution but a cultural one—it preserves the rural communities, traditional knowledge, and landscape biodiversity that industrial and synthetic alternatives would erase. The food system of the future should be diverse rather than monolithic, drawing on indigenous land management practices and agroecological science rather than ceding control to startups whose primary motivation is market share, not planetary health.
texts.D: Our current obsession with finding a 'meat substitute' reveals how deeply meat-eating is embedded in our cultural identity. [40]While many focus on the technical aspects of lab-grown meat, I believe the biggest challenge will be the 'ick factor'—the deep-seated psychological resistance many people feel toward eating food grown in a vat.[/40] We cannot simply engineer our way out of thousands of years of evolutionary preference. [37]However, I am more optimistic than others about the potential for 'hybrid' diets—part plant, part cultivated—to bridge the gap.[/37] A gradual, pleasure-oriented transition—one that celebrates the craft of cooking and elevates the eating experience—is far more likely to achieve lasting behavioral change than moralistic campaigns that shame consumers for their dietary choices. We should be aiming for a 'less but better' approach, valuing quality over cheap, mass-produced protein. Gastronomy has always evolved alongside technology, and the most successful food revolutions have offered people something genuinely delicious, affordable, and aligned with their values.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the role of lab-grown meat as a solution to meat consumption?
questions.explanation: B believes lab-grown meat is the 'only realistic way' to satisfy global demand. A, however, sees it as a 'technological distraction' and argues for the 'simpler' path of veganism.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B regarding the environmental impact of all forms of livestock farming?
questions.explanation: B implies meat production generally involves 'environmental degradation.' C argues that 'well-managed livestock can actually restore soil health and sequester carbon,' seeing it as potentially beneficial for the environment.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's skepticism about the morality of high-tech meat production?
questions.explanation: A argues that we need to rethink our relationship with species rather than finding 'better ways' to produce meat. C shares this skepticism, doubting the 'moral superiority' of lab meat which 'detaches us even further from nature.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B on what constitutes the primary obstacle to the success of lab-grown meat?
questions.explanation: B states that the 'real hurdle' is the 'cost.' D disagrees, stating that the 'biggest challenge' will be the 'ick factor'—the 'psychological resistance' of consumers.
